script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'pass7-confirm2',
  description: 'Pass-7: confirm the consolidated operator-identity PII remediation (J-002 concern + J-008)',
  phases: [{ title: 'Confirm' }],
}

const REPO = '/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay'

const SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  additionalProperties: false,
  required: ['verdict', 'appropriate', 'complete', 'rationale', 'concerns'],
  properties: {
    verdict: { type: 'string', enum: ['CONFIRMED', 'CONCERN', 'REJECTED'] },
    appropriate: { type: 'boolean' },
    complete: { type: 'boolean' },
    rationale: { type: 'string' },
    concerns: { type: 'array', items: { type: 'string' } },
  },
}

const DESC = `Consolidated operator-identity PII remediation in commit 7c50440 on branch harden/pass-7, addressing BOTH:
(a) J-002 confirm-concern: the prior full-name scrub ("[REDACTED]") left the operator's BARE first/last name in cleartext governance prose (256x "[REDACTED] approves ...", "[REDACTED] builds and governs ...").
(b) J-008 (new, caught by the pass-7 privacy coverage matrix): the operator GitHub handle "[REDACTED]" leaked across 193 transcript files (github.com/<handle> URLs, "gh --repo <handle>/..." commands) — PII no email rule (needs @) or username rule (home dir is "[REDACTED]") covered.

THE FIX (scripts/capture_transcripts.py):
1. _display_name_patterns(): in addition to the full-name pattern, now also emits a per-TOKEN pattern for each name token with len>=4 (e.g. "[REDACTED]\\b", "[REDACTED]\\b") — NO leading \\b (handles JSON-\\n-glued names), trailing \\b (protects "Joneses"). So the bare first/last name is redacted standalone.
2. _repo_handle_patterns() (NEW): derives the repo owner from "git remote -v" (regex github.com[:/]<owner>/) + the ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES env; redacts each handle (len>=4) as a bare token everywhere (URLs, gh args, <handle>@ email stem). Repo name is preserved (legitimate provenance).
3. Both applied in _scrub after the username rule.
4. The committed corpus was RE-SCRUBBED in place (207 files rewritten).
Regression tests: test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens, test_scrub_redacts_github_handle (in tests/test_scripts.py).`

function prompt(lens) {
  return `You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer ${lens}) for the hc-assay production-hardening campaign, repo at ${REPO} (branch harden/pass-7).

${DESC}

Read the actual changed file ${REPO}/scripts/capture_transcripts.py and the tests. You may run tests: cd ${REPO} && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q tests/test_scripts.py  (use "python -m pytest", NOT bare pytest).

CRITICALLY, independently verify the COMMITTED CORPUS is actually clean — run grep yourself, e.g.:
  cd ${REPO} && grep -rl "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l
  cd ${REPO} && grep -rl "[REDACTED]" transcripts/ | wc -l
  cd ${REPO} && grep -rloE "[REDACTED][^e]" transcripts/ | wc -l
A passing unit test does NOT prove the committed transcripts are clean — verify both.

Decide:
- appropriate: right root cause, right layer, sound approach, acceptable over-redaction trade-off (the corpus has no unrelated bearer of "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]"; confirm or refute by sampling contexts).
- complete: corpus actually clean (grep ~0 for operator name tokens + handle, ignoring the bare provider word "protonmail" in meta-discussion which is not PII), regression tests discriminate (would fail if reverted), and no obvious remaining operator-identity vector.
Return CONFIRMED only if both true. CONCERN if appropriate but a real residual gap remains (name a concrete one). REJECTED if wrong/non-discriminating. List concrete concerns; empty array if none.`
}

phase('Confirm')
const votes = (await parallel([
  () => agent(prompt('A'), { label: 'confirm:privacy:A', phase: 'Confirm', schema: SCHEMA }).then((v) => ({ lens: 'A', ...v })),
  () => agent(prompt('B'), { label: 'confirm:privacy:B', phase: 'Confirm', schema: SCHEMA }).then((v) => ({ lens: 'B', ...v })),
])).filter(Boolean)

const bothConfirmed = votes.length === 2 && votes.every((v) => v.verdict === 'CONFIRMED')
log(`PRIVACY REMEDIATION (J-002 concern + J-008): ${bothConfirmed ? 'BOTH-CONFIRMED' : 'NEEDS-ATTENTION'} — ${votes.map((v) => v.lens + ':' + v.verdict).join(', ')}`)
return { bothConfirmed, votes }

votes.rationale:
The fix targets the right root cause at the right layer (scripts/capture_transcripts.py _scrub), and the approach is sound: per-token name redaction (#J-002 confirm-concern) and a new _repo_handle_patterns deriving the owner from git remote + ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES (#J-008). The regression tests are discriminating — test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens asserts "[REDACTED] approves" is gone (only the new per-token loop achieves this; the full-name "[REDACTED]" pattern would not match), and test_scrub_redacts_github_handle asserts the handle is gone (only the new pattern achieves this); both would fail if reverted. All 13 tests pass. The over-redaction trade-off is acceptable (token guard len>=4, trailing \b protects "Joneses"). The capitalized targets are clean: grep for "[REDACTED]"=0, "[REDACTED]"=0, "[REDACTED][^e]"=0, "[REDACTED]"=0.

However the committed corpus is NOT actually clean — the completeness claim fails. The name-token and handle patterns are CASE-SENSITIVE (re.escape(t) + r"\b", no re.IGNORECASE), so lowercased operator-identity tokens survived the in-place re-scrub. Concrete residuals (verified by grep, excluding self-referential meta-discussion of the scrub logic): "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" as an initiated_by field in 4 files (also dodges the email rule because "@hcgrc" has no TLD dot), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human)" in an actor table, standalone lowercase "[REDACTED]" in a real actor table, and the handle-derived fragment "[REDACTED]-audience-profiles" (skill name) in 8 files. The lowercase first name + hyphenated full name re-identify the operator just as the capitalized forms did — the same class of PII the fix was meant to close, just in a different case.
concerns: Case-sensitivity gap: name-token and full-name patterns use re.escape(t)+r'\b' with no re.IGNORECASE, so the lowercased operator first name '[REDACTED]' survives in the committed corpus — '[REDACTED]@hcgrc' appears as an initiated_by field in 4 transcript files (transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-.../subagents/workflows/wf_82001839-351/agent-a18107c97bdfa2626.jsonl and 3 others). This is the same first-name PII the #J-002 confirm-concern flagged, just lowercased.

Lowercase hyphenated full name '[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]' (human) survives in an actor table in transcripts/.../wf_82001839-351/agent-a61cd08ca3c6d93a2.jsonl, plus standalone lowercase '[REDACTED]' in real (non-meta) actor-table contexts in wf_574c42b7 and wf_cdf1b8e2 transcripts — not matched by the case-sensitive token pattern.

'[REDACTED]@hcgrc' is not covered by the email rule either, because the email regex requires a '\.[A-Za-z]{2,}' TLD and 'hcgrc' has no dot — so neither the email rule nor the new name-token rule redacts this operator email-stem.

Handle-fragment leak: the handle pattern redacts the full remote owner '[REDACTED]' but not the bare substring '[REDACTED]', leaving '[REDACTED]-audience-profiles' (a skill name embedding the handle minus its 'the' prefix) in cleartext across 8 transcript files. Borderline identity-adjacent rather than a hard handle leak, but it is the operator handle stem.

Recommended fix: add re.IGNORECASE to the display-name token patterns (or lowercase-normalize), and add a TLD-less email-stem rule or include '[REDACTED]' as a case-insensitive token; then re-scrub the corpus in place again and re-run the same greps to confirm zero (excluding the scrub's own self-referential meta-discussion of 'Joneses'/'the Joneses live here').

votes.rationale: The fix targets the right root cause at the right layer (scripts/capture_transcripts.py _scrub, applied after the username rule) with a sound approach, and the named targets are genuinely clean in the committed corpus: capitalized standalone "[REDACTED]" = 0, "[REDACTED]" (excluding the deliberately-protected "Joneses") = 0, full handle "[REDACTED]" = 0 (case-sensitive and case-insensitive), and surviving github.com/<owner> URLs are all unrelated third-party orgs (anthropics, astral-sh, actions, az9713, etc.), not the operator. The regression tests (test_scrub_redacts_bare_name_tokens, test_scrub_redacts_github_handle) are discriminating — they assert the bare-token redaction, the trailing-\b "Joneses" protection, the handle-everywhere redaction, and the repo-name preservation, all of which fail if reverted; all 13 tests pass. Over-redaction trade-off is acceptable: "[REDACTED]" residuals are essentially all the protected "Joneses" surname or scrub-meta-discussion, and the corpus has no unrelated bearer of these tokens. HOWEVER the corpus is NOT fully clean of operator-identity PII, so I cannot confirm "complete." A case-insensitive sweep surfaced the operator's LOWERCASE identity surviving in committed transcripts, which the case-sensitive re.escape("[REDACTED]")/("[REDACTED]") patterns and the TLD-requiring email regex both miss. Verified empirically by running _scrub against the actual leaked strings: "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" -> unchanged, "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human)" -> unchanged, while "[REDACTED] approves" -> "[REDACTED] approves". This is the same operator-identity vector J-002/J-008 set out to close, merely lowercased and used as an actor/handle token.
concerns: RESIDUAL LEAK (4 committed files): the operator's lowercase identity survives. `"initiated_by": "[REDACTED]@hcgrc"` and `[REDACTED]-[REDACTED] (human)` appear in transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d/subagents/workflows/wf_82001839-351/agent-a61cd08ca3c6d93a2.jsonl, agent-a18107c97bdfa2626.jsonl, agent-ad24f5b248f475d06.jsonl, and wf_049f05ea-b77/agent-af9e2478f7e163c25.jsonl. Empirically _scrub leaves both unchanged.

ROOT CAUSE of the lowercase gap: _display_name_patterns() compiles re.escape(token) with no re.IGNORECASE, so only the capitalized '[REDACTED]'/'[REDACTED]' forms are redacted; lowercase actor/handle forms ([REDACTED], [REDACTED]-[REDACTED]) pass through.

SECOND ROOT CAUSE: the email rule `[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@...\.[A-Za-z]{2,}` requires a dotted TLD, so the internal address `[REDACTED]@hcgrc` (no dot) is not matched by the email rule either — operator user@org actor identifiers of the form name@host (no TLD) are entirely uncovered.

Suggested remediation: emit the per-token name patterns (and ideally the username/handle patterns) with re.IGNORECASE, and re-scrub the corpus in place; optionally relax the email rule to also catch `<local>@<host>` without a TLD. After fixing, `grep -rliE '[REDACTED]@|[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]' transcripts/` should be 0.

Minor/borderline (not a leak, FYI): the literal fragment `thehipster…` (UTF-8 ellipsis 0xe280a6, truncated example) survives in one workflow file that documents the scrub work itself; it is not the full handle and not re-identifying, analogous to the accepted 'protonmail' provider-word exemption — does not by itself block confirmation.

logs: PRIVACY REMEDIATION (J-002 concern + J-008): NEEDS-ATTENTION — A:CONCERN, B:CONCERN
summary: Pass-7: confirm the consolidated operator-identity PII remediation (J-002 concern + J-008)
